name: files
context: Read, inspect, join, and export CSV, Parquet, JSON, globs, partitions, and remote files with DuckDB
info: DuckDB's file readers are the fastest path from raw data to useful SQL: agents can query CSV, Parquet, JSON, newline-delimited JSON, compressed files, HTTPS URLs, S3-style paths, file lists, and globs without loading data into a server first. This node covers `read_csv`, `read_parquet`, `read_json_auto`, filename and partition columns, `union_by_name`, `COPY` exports, and format-conversion workflows. Use it with [[duckdb/cli]] for shell automation, [[duckdb/python]] for DataFrame workflows, [[duckdb/secrets]] for object storage credentials, and [[duckdb/performance]] for large datasets.
triggers: load a folder of csv files

sections.title: CSV reads
sections.body: Use `read_csv` for delimited files. Important options include `header`, `delim` or `sep`, `columns` for explicit schema, `types` or `dtype` depending on surface, `sample_size`, `ignore_errors`, `nullstr`, `dateformat`, `timestampformat`, `union_by_name`, and `filename`. CSV auto-detection is useful but should be pinned for production pipelines.

sections.title: Parquet reads
sections.body: Use `read_parquet` or direct `'file.parquet'` scans for columnar data. Parquet supports column pruning, predicate pushdown, row groups, compression, globs, lists of files, and remote reads through httpfs. Prefer Parquet for repeated analytics and large intermediate outputs.

sections.title: JSON and JSON Lines
sections.body: Use `read_json_auto` or `read_json` for regular JSON, arrays of objects, newline-delimited JSON, and compressed JSON files. Inspect inferred nested types before flattening. For unstable JSON, project only needed fields and cast deliberately.

sections.title: Multiple files and schema drift
sections.body: DuckDB accepts glob patterns like `dir/*.parquet`, recursive globs like `dir/**/*.parquet`, and file lists like `read_parquet(['a.parquet', 'b.parquet'])`. Use `union_by_name = true` when file schemas differ or column order is not guaranteed. Use `filename = true` for lineage.

sections.title: COPY imports and exports
sections.body: `COPY table TO 'file.csv'` exports tables; `COPY (SELECT ...) TO 'file.parquet' (FORMAT parquet)` exports query results. Use `CREATE TABLE AS SELECT` when you want persistent DuckDB tables. Prefer `COPY` to stdout only for small outputs; large exports should go to files.

sections.title: Remote files
sections.body: Remote HTTPS and S3-style paths require httpfs support and often secrets. Core extension autoload can make simple reads work, but production scripts should explicitly document `INSTALL httpfs`, `LOAD httpfs`, and `CREATE SECRET`. See [[duckdb/secrets]] for credential patterns.

links.context: Use the same readers from Python
Configure credentials for S3-compatible file reads

related.note: The CLI scripts use these file readers for inspection and conversion
Python exposes the same read_csv/read_parquet/read_json capabilities
For database dumps and restore use database-native backup tools, not DuckDB file scans

author: dojo-community
